memes.label: Abraham
memes.description: Biblical patriarch and founding figure of Judaism, Christianity, and Islam — whose divine calling and covenant Holbach treats as an exemplar of supernatural credulity and whose story reveals the human origins of revealed religion.

memes.label: Absolution
memes.description: The priestly power to forgive sins — Holbach exposes absolution as an invented mechanism that encourages crime by promising cheap reconciliation with God, serving the interests of the priesthood rather than morality.

memes.label: Absurdity
memes.description: That which violates reason so flagrantly that no rational person can accept it — Holbach applies this term to religious doctrines, miracles, mysteries, and the morality derived from them.

memes.label: Accident
memes.description: Holbach denies the reality of accident or chance, arguing that every event has determinate causes — what we call "accident" is simply an effect whose cause we do not yet know.

memes.label: Adam
memes.description: The biblical first man whose disobedience in Eden, according to Christian doctrine, brought sin and death upon all humanity — a narrative Holbach cites as an example of the irrationality and moral incoherence of revealed religion's account of human origins.

memes.label: Almighty
memes.description: Holbach's critique of the attribute of divine omnipotence — the claim that God is almighty is refuted by the observable imperfection of creation, the existence of evil, and the failure of divine projects including Christianity itself.

memes.label: Alms
memes.description: Charitable giving prescribed by religion, critiqued by Holbach as a mechanism that transforms wealth extracted from the people into a display of piety — substituting charity for justice while enriching the clergy.

memes.label: Altar
memes.description: Holbach uses the altar as a symbol of the alliance between religious and political power — the "throne and altar" partnership that sustains despotism by sanctifying earthly authority with divine sanction. (002-423)(APP007-026)

memes.label: Angel
memes.description: Supernatural intermediary beings in Christian theology that Holbach treats as mythological constructs — evidence that religion populates the universe with imaginary entities to explain what it cannot understand.

memes.label: Apostle
memes.description: Holbach examines the early Christian apostles as founders and reformers who attracted disciples; he also redeploys the term positively, calling on enlightened individuals to become "apostles of reason." (002-258)(002-412)

memes.label: Arabia
memes.description: Arabia as the geographical origin of Islam in Holbach's "Common Sense"; the "Arabians, encouraged by their Divine preceptor, carried the sword and the..." exemplifies Holbach's critique of religious violence — a faith born in the Arabian desert that, like Christianity, spread through conquest rather than persuasion.

memes.label: Ascension of Jesus
memes.description: The Ascension of Jesus, the miracle by which Christ is said to have risen bodily to heaven; critiqued in Holbach's "Common Sense" through exposure of contradictory Gospel accounts and the silence of key witnesses.

memes.label: Atheism
memes.description: The absence of belief in deities; in Holbach's "Common Sense," the rational position of one who relies on nature and reason rather than supernatural claims.

memes.label: Atheist
memes.description: One who denies the existence of God — a category that Holbach reclaims from theological condemnation, arguing that the atheist is more honest and often more virtuous than the believer whose piety masks cruelty.

memes.label: Atonement
memes.description: The doctrine that Christ's death satisfies divine justice for human sin — Holbach treats atonement as a morally incoherent invention that makes God a tyrant demanding blood payment, contradicting both reason and any notion of benevolent deity.

memes.label: Attribute
memes.description: The qualities or properties ascribed to God by theologians, which Holbach argues are mutually contradictory and thus demonstrate the impossibility of the divine being.

memes.label: Authority
memes.description: The claimed right of religious institutions and their officers to command belief and obedience, which Holbach argues is illegitimate because it rests on deception rather than demonstrated competence or consent.

memes.label: Babylon
memes.description: The ancient Mesopotamian city and empire whose conquest of Jerusalem and destruction of the Temple in 586 BCE Holbach treats as empirical refutation of the claim that Judea was divinely protected as God's chosen nation.

memes.label: Baptism
memes.description: A Christian sacrament of ritual purification by water — which Holbach treats as a meaningless ceremony, and whose gospel accounts reveal irreconcilable contradictions in the New Testament narrative.

memes.label: St. Bartholomew's Day Massacre
memes.description: The mass killing of Huguenots in Paris on August 24, 1572, cited by Holbach as the paradigmatic example of religious fanaticism producing atrocity — a crime committed as an act of piety.

memes.label: Baruch Spinoza
memes.description: "Dutch rationalist philosopher (1632–1677), author of Ethics, praised by Holbach as a modern follower of Epicurean materialism."

memes.label: Bayle
memes.description: Pierre Bayle (1647–1706), French philosopher and skeptic whose works Holbach quotes approvingly and whom he groups with Hobbes and Spinoza among the modern critics of religion. (002-225)(002-448)

memes.label: Being
memes.description: Holbach examines the concept of "being" as applied to divinity, arguing that the notion of a supreme being is an incoherent, self-contradictory construct born of human imagination and fear, not reason.

memes.label: Belief
memes.description: The mental acceptance of propositions without or against evidence, which Holbach identifies as the psychological foundation of religious error, sustained by habit, fear, and social pressure rather than rational conviction.

memes.label: Bethlehem
memes.description: Bethlehem, a town in Judea identified in the Gospels as the birthplace of Jesus; Holbach's "Common Sense" cites Herod's massacre of Bethlehem's children as an atrocity that disproves divine providence and exposes Gospel unreliability.

memes.label: Bible
memes.description: The Bible as an object of Holbach's rational critique; its contradictions, historical unreliability, and textual corruption as evidence against claims of divine inspiration.

memes.label: Blame
memes.description: In Holbach's theodicy critique, the concept of blaming God for human evil reverses the theological narrative — if God created human nature, then God, not humanity, bears responsibility for the evils that follow from it. (002-116)

memes.label: Blaspheme
memes.description: Holbach argues that the very concept of blasphemy is incoherent — a God who could be offended or blasphemed would be a contradictory being, and the real function of blasphemy laws is to protect priestly authority rather than divine dignity. (002-107)

memes.label: Blasphemy
memes.description: Holbach's analysis of blasphemy as a category without coherent content — the true blasphemers are the theologians who attribute cruelty to God, and the cry of "blasphemy" is a mechanism for suppressing rational criticism of religious claims.

memes.label: Blessing
memes.description: Holbach's critique of priestly blessings as empty rituals that claim supernatural efficacy — he argues that blessings redirect gratitude from natural and human causes toward an imaginary benefactor while enriching the clergy who dispense them.

memes.label: Body
memes.description: Holbach defends a thoroughgoing materialism in which the body is the whole of the human being; the notion of an immaterial soul distinct from and superior to the body is an absurdity born of ignorance and exploited by priests.

memes.label: canon
memes.description: The Catholic practice of canonization — declaring a deceased person a saint — which Holbach dismisses as accompanied by fabricated miracles, such as the bells of Lisbon ringing by themselves on St. Antoine de Padua's canonization day.

memes.label: Capernaum
memes.description: A fishing village on the northern shore of the Sea of Galilee, identified in the Gospels as the center of Jesus's Galilean ministry — cited by Holbach as one of the towns whose miraculous works were allegedly witnessed yet which failed to produce universal conversion, undermining the claim that miracles compel belief.

memes.label: Catechism
memes.description: Holbach identifies the catechism as the primary mechanism for transmitting religious dogma to children — its first question, he argues, inevitably concerns the duty of paying priests rather than moral conduct or rational inquiry. (002-017)

memes.label: Catholic Church
memes.description: The Catholic Church, the largest Christian denomination; the primary institutional target of critique in Holbach's "Common Sense."

memes.label: Cause
memes.description: The principle of necessary connection between events in nature, which Holbach uses to reject the theological "First Cause" and establish a deterministic, materialist universe.

memes.label: Ceremony
memes.description: Holbach denounces religious ceremonies as puerile, often barbaric practices that substitute external ritual for genuine morality; they serve priestly authority while contributing nothing to human welfare or enlightenment.

memes.label: Certainty
memes.description: The degree of confidence warranted by evidence — Holbach contrasts the genuine certainty produced by sensory experience and reason with the false certainty of faith, arguing that the things of this life have a certainty that religious promises can never match.

memes.label: Chance
memes.description: Chance as a word that masks human ignorance of natural causes — Holbach argues that nothing occurs by chance in a universe governed by necessary laws, and that the concept is an intellectual abdication.

memes.label: Charity
memes.description: Holbach's critique of Christian charity as a hollow substitute for genuine justice and good governance, which perpetuates misery rather than preventing it.

memes.label: Charlatan
memes.description: The religious impostor who exploits credulity for power and profit — Holbach identifies the charlatan as the archetype behind all priestly authority, reducing the divine spokesman to a calculating deceiver who manipulates the fearful and ignorant.

memes.label: "Chimera"
memes.description: "In Holbach's materialist philosophy, chimeras are imaginary constructs with no corresponding reality — a category into which he places all theological concepts, spiritual substances, and supernatural entities."

memes.label: china
memes.description: China as Holbach's strategic empirical counter-example — an ancient civilization demonstrating that morality and social order flourish without Christian revelation, exposing the parochialism of universal divine dispensation.

memes.label: choice
memes.description: Free choice — which Holbach denies, arguing that man's birth, will, and actions are all determined by forces beyond his control; he is "not free in his choice." (002-134)

memes.label: chosen-people
memes.description: The doctrine of Israel's exclusive divine election, subjected by Holbach to sustained rational critique as a provincial mythology incompatible with the idea of a universal God.

memes.label: Christianity
memes.description: The religion centered on Jesus Christ, which Holbach identifies as the primary target of his critique — a system of "incomprehensible theology," "ridiculous fables," and "puerile ceremonies" that has brought more harm than good to humanity.

memes.label: Church
memes.description: The institutional structure of organized Christianity; Holbach analyzes the Church in "Common Sense" as a human power structure that exploits the concept of God to extract wealth, obedience, and political influence from credulous populations.

memes.label: Cicero
memes.description: Roman statesman and philosopher (106–43 BCE) whom Holbach approvingly quotes on divine goodness.

memes.label: Clergy
memes.description: The professional class of religious functionaries who mediate between humanity and the divine; Holbach analyzes the clergy in "Common Sense" as a self-perpetuating elite whose power depends on maintaining popular ignorance about the true nature of religion.

memes.label: Concept Citations
memes.description: The 50 most cross-referenced concept pages in the wiki, ranked by total backlink count — revealing which philosophical and theological ideas from Holbach's Common Sense form the densest nodes in the knowledge network.

memes.label: Concept Index
memes.description: Alphabetical index of all concept entries in the wiki — philosophical ideas, theological doctrines, and analytical categories from Holbach's Common Sense and Enlightenment thought.

memes.label: Confession
memes.description: Holbach's critique of auricular confession as a mechanism of priestly surveillance and financial extraction — he argues it subverts true morality by teaching that sins against men can be pardoned by God through priestly mediation.

memes.label: Conscience
memes.description: The internal faculty of moral judgment, which Holbach argues is not a divine implant but a product of experience, education, and social conditioning — easily manipulated by religious authorities to justify cruelty.

memes.label: Consent
memes.description: In Holbach's rhetorical arsenal, the demand that one accept the logical consequences of one's beliefs — applied to mortality, divine justice, and free will.

memes.label: Contradiction
memes.description: A logical incompatibility between two propositions that cannot both be true — Holbach identifies contradiction as the defining feature of theological discourse and the surest sign of error.

memes.label: Conversion
memes.description: Holbach treats religious conversion with deep skepticism, arguing that sincere conversions are exceedingly rare and that most conversions are politically motivated or enforced by violence rather than by genuine conviction. (002-377)(APP007-070)

memes.label: Corruption
memes.description: The moral and institutional decay that Holbach identifies as the necessary consequence of religious systems — priests corrupt truth, tyrants corrupt virtue, and theological doctrines corrupt the natural moral sense

memes.label: Council of Trent
memes.description: The 19th ecumenical council of the Catholic Church (1545–1563), convened in response to the Protestant Reformation; the Council of Trent defined Catholic doctrine on scripture, original sin, justification, and the sacraments, and is cited in "Common Sense" as a doctrinal authority representing the institutional church's claim to define truth by decree rather than reason.

memes.label: Courage
memes.description: Courage as a natural virtue in Holbach's ethics; Meslier praised as the first priest with "the courage and the honesty to abjure religious errors" — intellectual courage as the precondition of enlightenment.

memes.label: Covenant
memes.description: Holbach's critique of the biblical covenant — the claim that God made an everlasting pact with the Jewish people is empirically refuted by their historical condition of exile and dispossession, exposing the covenant as a failed divine promise.

memes.label: Creation
memes.description: Holbach argues that the universe was not created — matter is eternal and moves by its own inherent energy, and the doctrine of divine creation is both logically incoherent and empirically unsupported.

memes.label: Creator
memes.description: The theological concept of God as the maker of the universe — which Holbach systematically refutes through the problem of evil, the argument from imperfect design, and the moral incoherence of a perfect being who creates flawed and suffering creatures.

memes.label: Credulity
memes.description: The disposition to believe without examination, which Holbach identifies as the psychological foundation of all religion and the primary tool by which priests maintain their authority over the ignorant.

memes.label: Creed
memes.description: A formal statement of religious belief whose mandatory acceptance is critiqued by Holbach as a mechanism of intellectual coercion — creeds compel assent to incomprehensible propositions under threat of eternal punishment.

memes.label: Crime
memes.description: In Holbach's deterministic philosophy, crime is not a product of free will but of social conditions, bad education, and corrupt institutions — to punish crime without addressing its causes is both unjust and ineffective.

memes.label: Cruelty
memes.description: The infliction of suffering without just cause — which Holbach demonstrates is the logical consequence of theological doctrines of hell and divine punishment, and the historical constant of religious persecution conducted in God's name.

memes.label: Crusades
memes.description: The Crusades (1095–1291), a series of religious wars sanctioned by the papacy; cited in Holbach's "Common Sense" as evidence that religion unchains human passions and causes immense bloodshed.

memes.label: Custom
memes.description: Holbach identifies custom as the principal mechanism by which religious prejudices are transmitted across generations without rational examination; custom substitutes inherited habit for critical thought, perpetuating error under the guise of tradition.

memes.label: Damnation
memes.description: Holbach identifies the dogma of eternal damnation as one of the most pernicious theological inventions — a doctrine that turns God into a tyrant and makes religious faith a source of terror rather than consolation. (002-107)(002-285)

memes.label: Daniel
memes.description: The legendary hero of the biblical Book of Daniel, whose apocalyptic visions and miraculous deliverance from the lions' den Holbach treats as exemplars of the fabulous character of scriptural narrative.

memes.label: David
memes.description: Second king of Israel, author of the Psalms, and central figure in messianic prophecy — Holbach cites the Davidic lineage as a case study in the unreliability of biblical genealogy and the political function of messianic claims.

memes.label: David Hume
memes.description: "Scottish philosopher and historian (1711–1776), central figure of the Scottish Enlightenment, whose critique of miracles and theological reasoning is praised by Holbach in Common Sense."

memes.label: Death
memes.description: The cessation of organic life; Holbach argues that fear of death is the psychological root of religious belief and the doctrine of an immortal soul an absurdity unsupported by any evidence.

memes.label: Deist
memes.description: In Holbach's analysis, the deist occupies a middle ground between traditional religion and atheism — believing in a creator God but rejecting revealed religion and ecclesiastical authority. (002-330)

memes.label: Deity
memes.description: Holbach's critique of the concept of a deity — a contradictory, fear-generated abstraction whose attributes are mutually incompatible and whose social function is to legitimize priestly and political authority.

memes.label: Democritus
memes.description: The ancient Greek atomist philosopher whom Holbach names alongside Epicurus as a courageous predecessor who "dared to tear away the thick veil of prejudice" and deliver philosophy from theological fetters — an ancestor of the materialist tradition.

memes.label: Demon
memes.description: Malevolent supernatural beings in Christian theology that Holbach treats as mythological devices — invented to externalize evil and absolve God of responsibility for suffering.

memes.label: Design
memes.description: The theological argument that the order of the universe proves an intelligent designer; Holbach refutes it by showing that order is a necessary effect of natural causes, not evidence of divine purpose.

memes.label: Desire
memes.description: The natural impulse toward an object perceived as good; Holbach traces the origin of religion itself to the desire to dominate, and argues that human desires — properly understood — are the sole legitimate foundation of ethics.

memes.label: Despotism
memes.description: Arbitrary and absolute rule exercised without legal or moral restraint — Holbach argues that religion is the natural ally of despotism and that the despot is "a madman who injures himself" by alienating the people whose love is his true security.

memes.label: Devotion
memes.description: Devotion as a psychological disease of the imagination; Holbach diagnoses religious devotion as a pathology contracted in childhood and maintained by harmful remedies.

memes.label: Disciple
memes.description: Holbach examines discipleship as a model of uncritical submission to authority, citing the disciples of Pythagoras whose motto "He has said it" prefigures the credulity of religious believers. (002-274)

memes.label: disorder
memes.description: The state of confusion and chaos in the world, which Holbach uses as an argument against divine providence — if God is "the friend of order," why is the universe in disorder? (002-028)

memes.label: Divine Right
memes.description: The doctrine that monarchs derive their authority directly from God — Holbach exposes divine right as a political fiction serving the throne-and-altar alliance against the people.

memes.label: Divinity
memes.description: The concept of a supreme being or divine nature, which Holbach systematically critiques as a human projection — gods are made in the image of humans, not humans in the image of God.

memes.label: Doctrine
memes.description: An organized body of religious teachings, which Holbach subjects to rational scrutiny by demanding that doctrine be judged by its moral effects rather than by appeals to miracles, authority, or tradition.

memes.label: Dogma
memes.description: A religious doctrine proclaimed as incontrovertibly true by ecclesiastical authority, which Holbach critiques as a tool of intellectual subjugation designed to suppress reason and enforce priestly power.

memes.label: Dogmas
memes.description: Fixed religious doctrines imposed as absolute truth — critiqued by Holbach as products of ignorance and priestly invention that demand uncritical acceptance under threat of damnation

memes.label: Doubt
memes.description: The suspension of judgment in the face of insufficient evidence, which Holbach argues should, when rigorously applied to theological claims, lead not to permanent skepticism but to the certain conviction that a being of contradictory attributes cannot exist.

memes.label: Dreams
memes.description: Dreams and visions as the psychological origin of religious revelation; Holbach dismisses claimed divine communications as "impertinent dreams and impostures" arising from natural mental processes.

memes.label: Duty
memes.description: In Holbach's philosophy, duty is not derived from divine command but from the necessary relations among sensible beings living in society, grounded in human nature and the pursuit of well-being.

memes.label: Earth
memes.description: The physical world we inhabit — in Holbach's materialist cosmology, earth is a natural object governed by physical laws, not a divine creation, and the only home humanity will ever know.

memes.label: eden
memes.description: The Garden of Eden, or "terrestrial Paradise," which Holbach lists among biblical fables no more credible than pagan myths, citing the story of Eve formed from Adam's rib and a talking serpent.

memes.label: Edict of Nantes
memes.description: The Edict of Nantes (1598), granting religious toleration to French Protestants, and its Revocation (1685); the defining religious-political event of Holbach's France, whose consequences shaped the world in which "Common Sense" was written.
aliases: Revocation of the Edict of Nantes

memes.label: Education
memes.description: The systematic cultivation of reason and knowledge which Holbach presents as the indispensable remedy for superstition, tyranny, and human misery — the positive program that complements his negative critique of religion.

memes.label: Effect
memes.description: The necessary product of a cause, central to Holbach's deterministic materialism — every event in nature is the inevitable effect of antecedent causes acting according to fixed laws; there is no chance, no miracle, and no uncaused divine intervention.

memes.label: Egypt
memes.description: Ancient Egypt as Holbach's recurring anthropological example of primitive religion in "Common Sense"; the "wise Egyptians" who worshiped crocodiles, rats, and onions serve as the paradigm case of religion's absurd origins and the universality of superstitious worship across civilizations.

memes.label: Empire
memes.description: Empire as Holbach's metaphor for divine governance — a vast realm ruled by an invisible, contradictory monarch whose decrees are interpreted by competing priestly factions

memes.label: The Enlightenment
memes.description: The Enlightenment (c. 1685–1815), an intellectual movement emphasizing reason over tradition; the philosophical context in which Holbach wrote "Common Sense."

memes.label: Enthusiasm
memes.description: In Holbach's usage, "enthusiasm" denotes the blind, irrational zeal that religious belief induces — a state in which emotion overrides reason, enabling believers to prefer incomprehensible doctrines to demonstrable truths. (002-197)

memes.label: Epicurus
memes.description: "Ancient Greek philosopher (341–270 BCE), founder of Epicureanism, whose materialist atomism and formulation of the problem of evil are cited by Holbach as precursors to modern freethought."

memes.label: Epistemology Index
memes.description: Index of epistemological concepts in the wiki — Holbach's empiricist theory of knowledge and his critique of faith, revelation, and religious claims to truth.

memes.label: Equality
memes.description: The Enlightenment principle that nature places all beings on equal footing, deployed by Holbach to undermine theological hierarchy and argue for secular legal equality.

memes.label: Error
memes.description: The systematic production of false beliefs through theological indoctrination, which Holbach identifies as the mechanism by which religion perpetuates ignorance and prevents human progress.

memes.label: Essence
memes.description: The purported fundamental nature of a being, which Holbach argues is incoherent when applied to God — theologians simultaneously claim the divine essence is incomprehensible while assigning it specific attributes.

memes.label: Eternal
memes.description: The attribute of endless duration ascribed to God, which Holbach argues is meaningless when applied to an imperceptible being — eternity is a word without a referent, a theological placeholder for human ignorance about origins.

memes.label: Eternity
memes.description: Holbach argues that the concept of eternity, when applied to divine punishment, makes God a monster — infinite torment for finite offenses is the cruelest conceivable injustice, and no benevolent being could ordain it.

memes.label: Ethics
memes.description: The principles of moral conduct, which Holbach argues derive from natural reason and social necessity rather than divine command, in "Common Sense."

memes.label: Ethics Index
memes.description: Index of ethical and moral concepts in the wiki — Holbach's naturalistic ethics grounded in utility, self-interest, and social harmony rather than divine command.

memes.label: Europe
memes.description: The continent that was the setting for Holbach's critique of organized religion; Europe in the 18th century was simultaneously the most Christianized continent on Earth and the birthplace of the Enlightenment's systematic assault on Christianity's intellectual foundations.

memes.label: eve
memes.description: The biblical first woman as mythological fiction in Holbach's materialist critique — a narrative borrowed from Near Eastern sources rather than divinely revealed truth about human origins.

memes.label: Event Index
memes.description: Chronological index of all event entries — historical occurrences, religious events, councils, edicts, and watershed moments examined in Holbach's Common Sense and its historical context.

memes.label: Events & Places Index
memes.description: Combined index of event and place pages, ranked by total backlink count — a unified view of the historical occurrences and geographical locations referenced in Holbach's Common Sense.

memes.label: Evidence
memes.description: The empirical basis for belief that Holbach sets against faith — evidence from the senses and reason is the only reliable foundation for knowledge, and theology's demand for belief without evidence is a confession of its own falsity.

memes.label: Evil
memes.description: The existence of suffering and moral wrong in the world, which Holbach deploys as a decisive argument against the existence of a benevolent and omnipotent God.

memes.label: Excess
memes.description: Excess as the destructive extreme of human passion which Holbach argues is encouraged rather than restrained by religion — religious fanaticism and ascetic self-torment are themselves forms of excess.

memes.label: Exile
memes.description: The practice of exiling religious dissenters, cited by Holbach as evidence that religion fosters intolerance and cruelty in "Common Sense."

memes.label: Existence
memes.description: The state of being, which Holbach equates with material existence — all that exists is matter in motion, and immaterial substances or spiritual realms have no reality.

memes.label: Experience
memes.description: The accumulation of knowledge through sensory perception and practical engagement with the world, which Holbach presents as the sole foundation of all genuine understanding.

memes.label: Experiment
memes.description: In Holbach's empiricist epistemology, experiment — alongside observation and calculation — is a foundational method by which reason cultivates itself and arrives at truth, in direct opposition to the supernatural claims of religion. (002-425)

memes.label: Faith
memes.description: The acceptance of propositions without or against evidence; Holbach identifies faith as the epistemological engine of religious error, irreconcilable with reason and sustained only by habit, ignorance, and institutional pressure.

memes.label: Fanaticism
memes.description: In Holbach's analysis, religious fanaticism is a destructive psychological state in which devotion to a supposed divine cause overrides natural human sentiments, self-preservation, and moral judgment, leading to persecution, violence, and social disintegration.

relations.label: Revocation of the Edict of Nantes

memes.label: Fate
memes.description: In Holbach's materialist philosophy, fate is not a supernatural decree but the necessary outcome of natural laws — the same necessity regulates the fate of all sentient beings without regard to justice, merit, or divine will. (002-079)

memes.label: Fear
memes.description: The primary psychological emotion that Holbach identifies as the origin of religious belief — primitive humans, terrified by unexplained natural phenomena, invented gods to explain and appease the forces that threatened them.

memes.label: Festival
memes.description: A brief social gathering or celebration — in Holbach's usage, a metaphor for the fleeting nature of life and an argument that worldly pleasures should be enjoyed rather than renounced for an imaginary afterlife.

memes.label: Fiction
memes.description: Holbach's designation for religious doctrines that are human inventions presented as divine truths — fictions that serve priestly power while contradicting reason and experience.

memes.label: Finite
memes.description: The condition of bounded, limited existence which Holbach uses to argue that an infinite God cannot have any relation to finite human beings, rendering religion impossible.

memes.label: First Cause
memes.description: The theological concept of God as the initial uncaused cause of the universe, critiqued by Holbach as both logically unnecessary and contradicted by secondary natural causes (002-139).

memes.label: Folly
memes.description: The irrationality inherent in religious belief and practice; Holbach dissects folly as both a cause and a consequence of superstitious thinking, showing how religion systematically inverts reason.

memes.label: France
memes.description: Eighteenth-century France as the immediate political, social, and intellectual context of Holbach's "Common Sense"; the France of the ancien régime, where the alliance of throne and altar sustained a system of institutionalized religious control that Holbach's critique sought to dismantle.

memes.label: free-thought
memes.description: Holbach's ideal of intellectual liberation from priestly authority and religious dogma — the free exercise of reason against the forces of credulity that sustain organized religion.

memes.label: Free Will
memes.description: Holbach argues that free will is an idle fancy — human volition is determined by temperament, habit, education, and circumstance, and the illusion of freedom arises from ignorance of the causes that compel our choices.

memes.label: Galilee
memes.description: The northern region of ancient Palestine that serves as the primary setting for Jesus's ministry in the gospels — whose contradictory accounts, Holbach argues, demonstrate the unreliability of the New Testament as historical testimony.

memes.label: Genius
memes.description: The rare individual whose intellect and courage enable the discovery and proclamation of truths that challenge established errors — celebrated by Holbach as humanity's benefactors and persecuted by priestly power.

memes.label: God
memes.description: The central object of theological discourse and religious worship; Holbach subjects the concept of God to systematic rational critique in "Common Sense," arguing it is an incoherent, self-contradictory notion born of human ignorance and fear.

memes.label: Gods
memes.description: The plurality of deities across human cultures, which Holbach treats as empirical proof that gods are human inventions — every people creates gods in its own image, endowing them with local passions, tribal prejudices, and national characteristics.

memes.label: Goodness
memes.description: The quality of being benevolent and morally excellent, which Holbach argues is logically incompatible with the acts attributed to the God of orthodox theology — a being whose supposed goodness is contradicted by human suffering.

memes.label: Gospel
memes.description: The gospels as historically unreliable documents whose contradictory accounts and impracticable moral demands reveal their human rather than divine origin, a key argument in Holbach's critique of Christianity.

memes.label: Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz
memes.description: German rationalist philosopher and mathematician (1646–1716) whom Holbach names alongside Descartes and Malebranche as a philosopher "compelled to invent hypotheses and evasions" to reconcile his discoveries with religious dogma.

memes.label: Government
memes.description: The system of civil rule which Holbach argues should be based on reason and the social contract rather than on divine right — and which in practice has been corrupted by priestly influence and religious legitimation of tyranny.

memes.label: Grace
memes.description: Holbach argues that the theological doctrine of divine grace renders God an unjust tyrant who punishes creatures incapable of doing good without a gift He arbitrarily withholds.

memes.label: Gratitude
memes.description: The sentiment of thankful appreciation — which Holbach argues cannot rationally be owed to a God whose conduct toward humanity is, by any honest accounting, a record of suffering, neglect, and arbitrary cruelty.

memes.label: Guilt
memes.description: Guilt as a natural social and psychological phenomenon, not a mark of divine judgment; Holbach critiques theological guilt as a tool of clerical control.

memes.label: Habit
memes.description: The psychological mechanism by which beliefs are maintained without rational examination — Holbach identifies habit as the primary force sustaining religious credulity against the evidence of reason.

memes.label: Happiness
memes.description: The state of human well-being and satisfaction which Holbach argues is the natural goal of life, achievable through reason and justice rather than religious devotion.

memes.label: Heaven
memes.description: The promised posthumous reward of Christian theology, which Holbach critiques as a psychological bribe that distracts from earthly happiness and enables exploitation in the present life.

memes.label: Hell
memes.description: The doctrine of eternal postmortem punishment, which Holbach argues is the ultimate instrument of religious terror — a concept that contradicts divine goodness while serving priestly power by exploiting human fear.

memes.label: Heresy
memes.description: Religious belief or opinion that deviates from established orthodoxy — Holbach argues that the concept of heresy is a mechanism of priestly power, not a genuine theological category, and that "heresy" is simply what the dominant sect calls the beliefs of its rivals.

relations.label: Revocation of the Edict of Nantes

memes.label: Heretic
memes.description: In Holbach's analysis, the label "heretic" functions as a tool of religious persecution — applied to those who deviate from established doctrine — rather than a genuine theological category. (002-324)

memes.label: Hermit
memes.description: Holbach uses the figure of the hermit — particularly the dervis who undertakes a seventh pilgrimage to Mecca — as a narrative device to expose the self-deception of religious gratitude and the blindness of piety to the reality of suffering. (002-173)(002-360)

memes.label: Herod the Great
memes.description: Herod the Great (c. 72–4 BCE), Roman client king of Judea; Holbach's "Common Sense" cites Herod's massacre of Bethlehem children as evidence that the biblical God is either powerless or malevolent, and that the Gospel narratives are historically unreliable.

memes.label: Thomas Hobbes
memes.description: English materialist philosopher (1588–1679) whom Holbach quotes as calling theology "the kingdom of darkness" and names among the moderns who "followed the path of Epicurus."

memes.label: Honor
memes.description: Honor as a natural, social-psychological check on human behavior that Holbach argues is more effective than religious fear.

memes.label: Hope
memes.description: The expectation of future happiness — Holbach argues that the theological "hope of a heaven, of a future life, is but imaginary," a consoling fiction that distracts from the real task of improving human life on earth.

memes.label: Human Nature
memes.description: In Holbach's materialist philosophy, human nature is not a fallen or depraved condition requiring supernatural repair, but the necessary product of natural laws — and religious doctrine's demand to renounce it is both impossible and morally destructive. (002-243)

memes.label: Humanity
memes.description: Holbach's materialist conception of the human species as neither depraved nor privileged; humanity flourishes through reason, tolerance, and freedom from theological oppression.

memes.label: Hypocrite
memes.description: Holbach identifies the hypocrite as a recurring figure produced by religious systems — one who professes piety while engaging in vice, using religion as a cover for immorality. (002-388)

memes.label: Idol
memes.description: In Holbach's critical vocabulary, idols and idolatry represent the fabricated objects of religious worship that priests and rulers construct to dominate the credulous — "gigantic idols, whom they have raised to their own grandeur" and who must eventually be demolished. (002-408)

memes.label: Idolatry
memes.description: The worship of physical objects or images as gods — a category that Holbach argues applies not only to pagan religions but to all organized worship, including Christianity, whose rituals and objects of veneration are indistinguishable in principle from the idolatries they claim to condemn.

memes.label: Ignorance
memes.description: The absence of knowledge, which Holbach identifies as the primary cause of superstition and the foundation upon which religious authority is built and maintained.

memes.label: Illusion
memes.description: In Holbach's critique, religious illusions are seductive deceptions crafted by skillful impostors that present agreeable falsehoods in place of uncomfortable truths, preventing humanity from pursuing real happiness through reason. (002-246)

memes.label: Imagination
memes.description: The mental faculty that produces images and ideas not directly grounded in sensory experience, which Holbach identifies as the primary source of religious belief — a faculty that, when ungoverned by reason, populates the mind with chimeras, mysteries, and supernatural fictions.

memes.label: Immortality
memes.description: Holbach argues that the doctrine of the immortality of the soul is an absurd supposition built upon wishful thinking rather than evidence, and that it serves priestly power rather than human consolation.

memes.label: Immutability
memes.description: The theological doctrine that God is unchanging in essence, will, and character; in Holbach's "Common Sense," a self-contradictory attribute refuted by the observable instability of the world.

memes.label: Imposture
memes.description: The deliberate deception by which religious authorities fabricate divine revelations and manipulate believers — Holbach's central thesis that religion is a human invention imposed by cunning elites upon credulous populations.

memes.label: Incarnation
memes.description: The theological doctrine that God took human form in Jesus Christ — Holbach argues that the incarnation is no more reasonable than pagan myths of divine descent, and that once the initial absurdity of a creator God is accepted, no subsequent mystery can be rejected on rational grounds.

memes.label: Incense
memes.description: A fragrant substance burned as a religious offering — Holbach uses incense metonymically for religious worship itself, asking whether worshippers perceive the barbarous character of the God to whom they offer it (120-002).

memes.label: Incomprehensible
memes.description: In Holbach's critique, the theological claim that God is "incomprehensible" to human reason is a self-refuting paradox — theology asserts God's unknowability while simultaneously demanding belief in detailed divine attributes. (002-005)

memes.label: Incredulity
memes.description: In Holbach's analysis, incredulity — the refusal to believe — is treated by theologians as the greatest of crimes, punished by a God whose existence is precisely what the incredulous doubt; this circularity exposes the coercive logic of religious belief. (002-033)

memes.label: India
memes.description: India as Holbach's example of a non-Christian civilization in "Common Sense," used to illustrate the problem of religious pluralism and the injustice of Christian exclusivism; "the Indian does not believe one word of what He said to the Chinaman" exemplifies the geographical contingency of religious belief.

memes.label: Indulgence
memes.description: Holbach contrasts divine indulgence — the supposed forbearance of God toward sinners — with the real-world indulgence of human beings toward one another's ignorance and failings. (002-125)(002-292)

memes.label: Infallible
memes.description: In Holbach's critique of religion, the claim of infallibility — whether of scripture, clergy, or doctrine — is exposed as a mechanism for imposing unquestionable authority upon believers. (002-229)

memes.label: Infidel
memes.description: Holbach critiques the category of "infidel" as a rhetorical weapon used by religious authorities to demonize those who question doctrine; he observes that competing sects accuse each other of infidelity while practicing the same intolerance. (002-377)(002-396)

memes.label: Infinite
memes.description: The concept of unlimited, unbounded being which Holbach argues is incomprehensible to finite minds and, when ascribed to God, renders all religion impossible.

memes.label: Infinity
memes.description: The concept of infinity as an attribute of God, critiqued by Holbach as a meaningless abstraction beyond human comprehension in "Common Sense."

memes.label: Injustice
memes.description: The violation of moral order that theology attributes to a just God — Holbach argues that the existence of injustice in the world is incompatible with the existence of a just and omnipotent deity, making theodicy an exercise in contradiction.

memes.label: Inquisition
memes.description: The Inquisition, a system of ecclesiastical tribunals established by the Catholic Church to suppress heresy; cited in Holbach's "Common Sense" as evidence of religion's capacity for cruelty and intolerance.

memes.label: "Materialism as the Foundation of Holbach's Critique of Religion"
memes.description: "An analysis of how Holbach's materialist ontology — the claim that only matter exists and all phenomena are natural — serves as the philosophical foundation for every strand of his critique of religion in 'Common Sense.'"

memes.label: "Priestcraft: The Sociology of Religious Deception in Holbach's 'Common Sense'"
memes.description: "An analysis of Holbach's concept of priestcraft — the systematic manufacture of religious belief by an organized clerical class for the purpose of social and political control — as a foundational idea in the sociology of religion."

memes.label: "Religion and Political Power in Holbach's Thought"
memes.description: "A synthetic analysis of how Holbach's 'Common Sense' theorizes the alliance between religious authority and political power as a system of mutual reinforcement for controlling populations."

memes.label: inspiration
memes.description: Divine inspiration — the claim of receiving revelations from God — which Holbach dismisses as a product of vanity, noting that saints who claimed heavenly inspirations often disturbed society with their "dreams." (002-338)

memes.label: Intercession
memes.description: Holbach identifies the doctrine of priestly intercession as a core mechanism of clerical power — priests claim the ability to intercede with the Deity on behalf of the laity, transforming themselves into indispensable spiritual intermediaries. (002-360)

memes.label: Interest
memes.description: The motive of self-preservation and well-being that Holbach identifies as the natural foundation of all human action — properly understood, it leads to virtue and social harmony, not vice; religion disguises this truth by portraying self-interest as sinful while exploiting it for clerical power.

memes.label: Intolerance
memes.description: The refusal to permit beliefs or practices differing from one's own — Holbach identifies religious intolerance as the necessary consequence of revealed religion's claim to exclusive truth, arguing that a God who punishes unbelief in the next world authorizes believers to punish it in this one.

relations.label: Revocation of the Edict of Nantes

memes.label: Isaac
memes.description: Second of the three biblical patriarchs, son of Abraham and father of Jacob — whose near-sacrifice Holbach treats as indistinguishable from pagan myths and whose divine promises he demonstrates were never fulfilled.

memes.label: Isaac Newton
memes.description: "English mathematician and physicist (1643–1727), whose genius Holbach invokes to illustrate natural human intellectual inequality."

memes.label: Islam
memes.description: The monotheistic faith founded by Muhammad — Holbach treats Islam as confirmation of his comparative thesis that all revealed religions share the same structural features of priestly invention, prophetic imposture, and exploitation of human credulity.

memes.label: Israel
memes.description: The biblical people of Israel as a recurring reference in Holbach's critique of revealed religion — Holbach analyzes the Israelite narrative to expose the contradictions between divine election and the historical record, arguing that Israel's story exemplifies how priestly editors retrofit national history into sacred myth.

memes.label: Jacob
memes.description: Third of the three biblical patriarchs, renamed Israel after wrestling with an angel — whose visions and divine revelations Holbach dismisses as indistinguishable from delusion or imposture.

memes.label: Jansenists
memes.description: A Catholic reform movement within 17th–18th century France, centered at the Abbey of Port-Royal; the Jansenists emphasized Augustinian doctrines of grace, predestination, and human depravity, and were the theological adversaries of the Jesuits — a conflict that Holbach references in "Common Sense" to illustrate how religious factions render God mutually contradictory in order to justify their own systems.

memes.label: Jean Meslier
memes.description: Jean Meslier (1664–1733), French Catholic priest who posthumously authored a radical atheistic testament; the nominal author of "Common Sense" (published by Holbach).

memes.label: St. Jerome
memes.description: Church Father and translator of the Vulgate (c. 347–420 CE); Holbach cites Jerome's own testimony that the Scriptures had been "corrupted and falsified" by countless hands.

memes.label: Jerusalem
memes.description: Jerusalem, a city sacred to Judaism, Christianity, and Islam; referenced in Holbach's "Common Sense" as the setting of biblical events subjected to rational critique.

memes.label: Jesuits
memes.description: The Society of Jesus (Jesuits), a Catholic religious order founded in 1540; mentioned in Holbach's "Common Sense" as part of the clerical apparatus of religious deception.

memes.label: Jesus Christ
memes.description: Jesus Christ (c. 4 BCE – c. 30 CE), central figure of Christianity; in Holbach's "Common Sense," his miracles and divinity are subjected to radical critique.

memes.label: John Locke
memes.description: "English philosopher and physician (1632–1704), father of empiricism and a foundational figure of the Enlightenment, cited by Holbach as an exemplar of human intellectual achievement alongside Newton."

memes.label: John the Evangelist
memes.description: One of the twelve apostles, traditionally credited with the Fourth Gospel, three Epistles, and the Apocalypse — whose contradictory chronology Holbach contrasts with the synoptic Gospels to demonstrate the unreliability of the biblical texts.

memes.label: Joseph
memes.description: Son of Jacob and Rachel, sold into slavery by his brothers, who rose to become vizier of Egypt — Holbach cites his story as a case study in the moral and historical unreliability of biblical narrative.

memes.label: Flavius Josephus
memes.description: Jewish historian (c. 37–100 CE) cited by Holbach as key negative evidence — his silence on Jesus's miracles and the Massacre of the Innocents undermines Gospel credibility.

memes.label: judaism
memes.description: Judaism in Holbach's critical genealogy as a human artifact — the tribal religion of a provincial people whose scriptures bear all the marks of their human, cultural origin rather than divine authorship.

memes.label: Judea
memes.description: The historical-geographical region of ancient Palestine that forms the setting of the biblical narrative; Holbach references Judea in "Common Sense" as the political context of the Gospel story, invoking "Herod, king of Judea" to anchor biblical claims in documented history — and thereby expose their historical implausibility.

memes.label: Judgment
memes.description: Holbach treats judgment as an experience-based faculty and critiques divine judgment as a mechanism of fear rather than genuine moral evaluation.

memes.label: Julian the Apostate
memes.description: Roman emperor (r. 361–363 CE) and pagan philosopher whom Holbach ranks among the "profane" authors superior to the Bible.

memes.label: Jupiter
memes.description: Holbach contrasts the material Jupiter of ancient paganism with the abstract God of modern theology — Jupiter could act upon the world while the modern theological God is a sterile abstraction incapable of producing anything.

memes.label: Justice
memes.description: The principle of giving each their due, which Holbach argues cannot be attributed to God without contradiction and must be grounded in human reason and social institutions.

memes.label: Knowledge
memes.description: The empirical understanding of natural phenomena through reason and observation — Holbach contrasts useful knowledge of this world with the useless "knowledge" of an incomprehensible God that theology claims to provide.

memes.label: Law
memes.description: In Holbach's philosophy, law is understood as the necessary relations arising from the nature of things, not as divine command — secular morality grounded in human sensibility renders supernatural legislation redundant.

memes.label: Leucippus
memes.description: Ancient Greek philosopher (5th century BCE), founder of atomic theory, whom Holbach names alongside Democritus and Epicurus among the Greeks who "dared to tear away the thick veil of prejudice" and deliver philosophy from theology.

memes.label: liberality
memes.description: Divine liberality — the boundless generosity attributed to God, which Holbach illustrates through a hermit who praises Allah's benefactions before discovering a battlefield of a hundred thousand corpses.

memes.label: Liberty
memes.description: The capacity to act according to one's own will, which Holbach argues is contradicted by theological doctrines of predestination and divine foreknowledge, while being the essential condition for moral responsibility.

memes.label: Life
memes.description: Holbach argues that the dogma of "another life" is a groundless fiction that serves the interests of priests at the expense of human happiness in this, the only life we can know, and that materialism offers a more consoling philosophy than the threat of eternal torment.

memes.label: Lisbon
memes.description: Lisbon as the site of the catastrophic 1755 earthquake that became a defining event of the European Enlightenment; Holbach references the "bells of Lisbon" ringing by themselves as an example of popular credulity in "Common Sense," while the earthquake's broader philosophical significance — undermining providential optimism — aligns with Holbach's systematic critique of divine benevolence.

memes.label: Love
memes.description: Holbach argues that sincere love of a terrifying, punitive God is psychologically impossible; genuine human affection arises from natural sensibility and mutual benefit, not from theological command.

memes.label: Luke
memes.description: The traditional author of the Third Gospel and the Acts of the Apostles — whose contradictions with Matthew's genealogy and geography, and whose narrative of the ascension, Holbach exploits to demonstrate the unreliability of the New Testament as historical record.

memes.label: Malebranche
memes.description: The French Cartesian philosopher and Oratorian priest whose theological compromises — inventing "hypotheses and evasions" to reconcile reason with revelation — Holbach cites as Exhibit A of how even the greatest minds are deformed by the demand to conform to religious dogma.

memes.label: Malice
memes.description: The attribution of malevolent intent to the deity, which Holbach argues is the inevitable consequence of any theology that posits an omnipotent God who permits evil.

memes.label: mandarins
memes.description: Chinese magistrates whom Holbach praises as "undeceived" about popular religion — they tolerate superstition for social peace but keep priests (bonzes) from disturbing the State. (002-280)

memes.label: Mark
memes.description: The traditional author of the earliest canonical Gospel — whose contradictions with the other evangelists on the resurrection appearances, and whose recognition by critical scholarship as a source for Matthew and Luke, Holbach uses to demonstrate the human compositional process behind the New Testament.

memes.label: Martyr
memes.description: Religious martyrs as victims of superstition manipulated by priestcraft; Holbach argues martyrdom proves fanaticism, not truth.

memes.label: Mary
memes.description: The mother of Jesus in the Christian tradition — whose virgin birth, divine maternity, and perpetual virginity Holbach treats as mythological patterns borrowed from pagan precedent rather than historical facts.

memes.label: Mass
memes.description: The Catholic Mass as a superstitious ceremony devoid of genuine meaning, critiqued by Holbach and rejected by Jean Meslier as "ridiculous administration."

memes.label: Materialism
memes.description: The philosophical position that all phenomena, including thought and consciousness, arise from matter and its motions; a central doctrine in Holbach's "Common Sense."

memes.label: Matter
memes.description: The physical substance of which all things consist; Holbach argues that matter is eternal, self-moving, and the sole source of all existence — no spiritual creator is needed to explain the world.

memes.label: Matthew
memes.description: One of the twelve apostles and the traditional author of the First Gospel — whose genealogy of Jesus Holbach contrasts with Luke's to demonstrate irreconcilable contradictions in the foundational documents of Christianity.

memes.label: Matthias the Apostle
memes.description: Matthias the Apostle — chosen to replace Judas Iscariot among the Twelve; an apocryphal gospel was attributed to him by early Christian sects.

memes.label: Mercy
memes.description: Holbach's critique of divine mercy as an arbitrary and morally incoherent concept — a God who punishes without mercy those He could have made virtuous contradicts any intelligible standard of justice.

memes.label: Merit
memes.description: The secular basis of moral evaluation — Holbach argues that merit is judged by conformity with human nature and contribution to social welfare, not by compliance with supernatural commands, and that theological systems of merit are arbitrary and harmful.

memes.label: Jean Meslier
memes.description: A French Catholic priest (1664–1729) whose posthumously discovered manuscript — a systematic atheist critique of religion — was published and circulated by Holbach, making Meslier the secret patron saint of radical Enlightenment anticlericalism.

memes.label: Messiah
memes.description: Holbach treats the concept of the Messiah with skepticism, noting that multiple claimants to the title existed and that Pascal's wager-like argument for belief fails to convince. (002-343)(APP007-008)

memes.label: Mind
memes.description: Holbach examines the human mind as a faculty systematically disabled by religious superstition, arguing that the mind, when freed from theological prejudice and allowed to reason from experience, is fully capable of understanding nature, morality, and the conditions of human happiness.

memes.label: Miracle
memes.description: A purported suspension of natural laws attributed to divine intervention, which Holbach systematically debunks as either misattributed natural phenomena or deliberate priestly fabrication.

memes.label: Missionaries
memes.description: Missionary zeal as self-interested ambition disguised as religious devotion; Holbach's materialist analysis of why preachers spread their doctrines at personal risk.

memes.label: Mode
memes.description: A modification or determinate state of substance, used by Holbach to explain particular beings as necessary expressions of nature rather than independent creations.

memes.label: Model
memes.description: In Holbach's philosophy, a model is an ideal pattern for thought or conduct — but he argues that religious models (saints, ascetics, the "God-man" Christ) are pernicious, and that humanity needs secular models grounded in reason, nature, and social utility.

memes.label: Mohammed
memes.description: The founder of Islam whose prophetic claim — like those of Moses and Jesus — Holbach treats as a humanly produced religious innovation, and whose rapid military success he cites as evidence that the spread of a religion depends on political power, not divine truth.

memes.label: Monarch
memes.description: The sovereign ruler of a state — Holbach uses the figure of the monarch as a central metaphor for divine governance and a key site of political critique, arguing that monarchs educated by priests inevitably become instruments of religious oppression rather than guardians of public welfare.

memes.label: Monk
memes.description: Holbach treats monks as the paradigmatic case of religious parasitism — men who live in idleness at the expense of society while claiming spiritual authority over it. (002-274)(002-360)

memes.label: Michel de Montaigne
memes.description: "French Renaissance philosopher and essayist (1533–1592), cited by Holbach as a skeptical authority on the fabulous character of religious narratives."

memes.label: Morality
memes.description: The system of principles governing right conduct; Holbach argues for a secular morality grounded in human nature and social utility, rejecting the claim that moral behavior requires religious belief.

memes.label: Moses
memes.description: The legendary Hebrew prophet and lawgiver traditionally credited with authorship of the Pentateuch; Holbach subjects the Mosaic narrative to historical and rational critique, questioning both its factual basis and its moral authority.

memes.label: Most Referenced Pages
memes.description: Ranking of the 50 most referenced entry pages in the wiki — a statistical view of which concepts, persons, and themes form the densest nodes in Holbach's Common Sense knowledge network.

memes.label: Motion
memes.description: Motion is inherent to matter, requiring no external motor; Holbach argues self-motion produces all natural phenomena, making divine intervention unnecessary.

memes.label: mysteries
memes.description: Religious mysteries as instruments of intellectual control in Holbach's analysis — deliberately incomprehensible doctrines that position clergy as sole interpreters and condition believers to accept absurdities without scrutiny.

memes.label: Mystery
memes.description: A contradiction or absurdity that theologians demand be accepted without examination; Holbach argues that mystery is the essence of religion — a deliberate device of priestcraft to disable rational scrutiny.

memes.label: Mystic
memes.description: Holbach critiques mystical interpretation as a strategy for evading rational scrutiny — religious texts are given "mystical and spiritual" meanings whenever their literal sense is indefensible. (002-249)(APP006-011)

memes.label: Nation
memes.description: The political and social collectivity that Holbach uses as evidence for the human origin of religion — every nation has invented its own gods, proving that gods are products of human imagination rather than universal revelation.

memes.label: Nations
memes.description: The collective human communities whose religious diversity Holbach deploys as empirical evidence that revelation is local and human-made — every nation has its own gods, proving that gods are products of national imagination rather than universal divine communication.

memes.label: Nature
memes.description: The totality of physical existence governed by immutable natural laws; Holbach's alternative to supernatural metaphysics, in which nature is self-sufficient and requires no divine creator or governor.

memes.label: Nazareth
memes.description: Nazareth, a town in Galilee, identified in the Gospels as the childhood home of Jesus; Holbach's "Common Sense" cites the conflicting Gospel accounts of Jesus's movements to and from Nazareth as evidence of the unreliability of Scripture.

memes.label: Necessity
memes.description: In Holbach's materialist philosophy, necessity is the principle that all events in nature are the inevitable results of prior causes acting according to fixed and invariable laws, leaving no room for chance, free will, or divine intervention.

memes.label: Nothingness
memes.description: Holbach deploys the concept of nothingness as a critical weapon against theological claims about God, creation ex nihilo, and the immortality of the soul; ultimate reality, he argues, is material and eternal — nothing comes from nothing and nothing returns to nothing.

memes.label: Oath
memes.description: A solemn appeal to a deity as witness to the truth of a statement or the sincerity of a promise — Holbach argues that the oath adds no real obligation beyond what human esteem and self-interest already provide.

memes.label: Obedience
memes.description: The religious demand for obedience to divine authority, critiqued by Holbach as a mechanism of theological control in "Common Sense."

memes.label: Obligation
memes.description: In Holbach's naturalistic ethics, moral obligation is not a command imposed by a divine lawgiver but a necessary relation arising from the nature of sensible beings living in society, binding them to actions that promote their own and others' well-being.

memes.label: Observation
memes.description: In Holbach's empiricist framework, observation — alongside experiment and calculation — is a foundational method of rational inquiry, contrasted with the groundless assertions of theology. (002-425)

memes.label: Offering
memes.description: Holbach treats religious offerings and sacrifices as a system of material exploitation — the clergy profit from the belief that divine favor can be purchased through gifts, prayers, and expiations. (002-241)(002-347)

memes.label: "Omnipotence"
memes.description: "The theological attribute of unlimited divine power, which Holbach argues is logically incompatible with the existence of evil, suffering, and the apparent failure of God's plans in history."

memes.label: Opinion
memes.description: The beliefs held without rational examination that Holbach identifies as the mechanism through which religion and despotism maintain control — opinion, when formed by authority rather than reason, becomes the prison of the mind; the cure is the free exercise of judgment.

memes.label: Order
memes.description: The regular, law-governed arrangement of the natural world, which Holbach argues is inherent to matter itself and requires no divine legislator to establish or maintain.

memes.label: Organization Citations
memes.description: All organization pages ranked by total backlink count — revealing which religious institutions, governing bodies, and social structures are most frequently discussed across Holbach's Common Sense.

memes.label: Organization Index
memes.description: Index of all organization entries — religious institutions, churches, monastic orders, and political bodies analyzed in Holbach's Common Sense and the Enlightenment critique of institutional religion.

memes.label: Origen of Alexandria
memes.description: Early Christian theologian (c. 185–253 CE); Holbach cites Origen as a Church Father who "corrupted the Scriptures" by imposing allegorical interpretations on texts that did not suit him.

memes.label: Original Sin
memes.description: The Christian doctrine that all humans inherit guilt from Adam's disobedience, critiqued by Holbach as an absurd theological fable that contradicts both justice and the observable character of human nature.

memes.label: Pagan
memes.description: The worshiper of multiple deities in antiquity — a category that Holbach strategically deploys to argue that Christianity is structurally identical to the paganism it claims to have superseded, differing only in the number of its gods.

memes.label: Paganism
memes.description: Paganism as a comparative lens in Holbach's critique; Holbach argues that Christian miracle claims and theological errors mirror pagan precedents, undermining any claim to unique divine revelation.

memes.label: Pain
memes.description: Holbach treats pain as a necessary feature of sentient existence that both refutes the idea of a benevolent God and reveals the cruelty of theological doctrines that sanctify suffering.

memes.label: Palestine
memes.description: The geographic region between the Mediterranean Sea and the Jordan River, sacred to Judaism, Christianity, and Islam — whose contested history Holbach uses to demonstrate that divine revelation is always local, never universal.

memes.label: Paradise
memes.description: Holbach's critique of paradise as an imaginary compensation for earthly suffering — he argues that the hope of a future heaven is a priestly fiction that reconciles men to injustice in the present world.

memes.label: Paris
memes.description: The capital of France and the epicenter of the European Enlightenment; Holbach's Paris salon was the meeting place of the most radical philosophers of the age, and the city itself was the stage on which the intellectual drama his work catalyzed would unfold politically in 1789.

memes.label: Blaise Pascal
memes.description: "French mathematician, physicist, and Christian apologist (1623–1662), author of Pensées, whose reasoning on miracles is refuted by Holbach in Chapter CXXX of Common Sense."

memes.label: Passion
memes.description: The natural drives and emotions that motivate human action; Holbach argues that passions are morally neutral — they can produce good or evil depending on how reason and society channel them — and that the noblest passion is the love of truth.

memes.label: Passions
memes.description: The passions as the necessary motor of human action in Holbach's materialist psychology — religion's failure to restrain them demonstrates the impotence of supernatural sanctions against natural causes

memes.label: Paul
memes.description: The apostle to the Gentiles and the most prolific author in the New Testament — whose epistles were rejected by some early Christian communities, whose theology transformed a Jewish messianic movement into a universal religion, and whose writings Holbach invokes in his survey of early Christian sectarian conflict over the canon.

memes.label: Penitent
memes.description: Holbach groups penitents — alongside hermits, fakirs, and fanatics — among those who embrace suffering as a religious duty, exemplifying the destructive doctrine that pleasing the Deity requires rendering oneself unhappy upon earth. (002-335)

memes.label: Perfection
memes.description: The attribute of complete flawlessness traditionally ascribed to God — Holbach argues that divine perfection is incompatible with the observable imperfections of the world and is a human projection rather than a discovered attribute.

memes.label: Persecution
memes.description: The systematic oppression of dissenters by religious authorities — which Holbach argues is the natural expression of theological intolerance and the inevitable consequence of priestly power.

relations.label: Revocation of the Edict of Nantes

memes.label: Person Citations
memes.description: All person pages ranked by total backlink count — revealing which historical figures, philosophers, and biblical characters are most frequently invoked across Holbach's Common Sense.

memes.label: Person Index
memes.description: Alphabetical index of all person entries in the wiki — historical figures, philosophers, theologians, and biblical characters discussed in Holbach's Common Sense and its intellectual milieu.

memes.label: Peter
memes.description: The chief of the twelve apostles and — according to Catholic tradition — the first Bishop of Rome, upon whose supposed primacy the papacy is founded, and whose miraculous relics and apocryphal literature Holbach invokes as evidence of the legendary accretion that characterizes Christian tradition.

memes.label: Phantom
memes.description: In Holbach's critique of religion, phantoms are imaginary beings — gods, demons, genii — created by priests to intimidate and control the credulous, filling their minds with terrors and preventing the exercise of reason. (002-033)

memes.label: Phenomena
memes.description: Holbach's empiricist appeal to observable natural phenomena against supernatural explanations — the visible world as the only legitimate object of knowledge.

memes.label: Philosophy
memes.description: The systematic pursuit of knowledge through reason and experience, which Holbach contrasts with theology as the genuine path to human wisdom — a "rational philosophy" that seeks to improve this life rather than prepare for an imaginary next one.

memes.label: Philosophy Index
memes.description: Index of philosophical concepts in the wiki — covering metaphysics, epistemology, ethics, and political philosophy from Holbach's materialist perspective.

memes.label: Pierre Bayle
memes.description: "French philosopher and Protestant critic (1647–1706), author of the Historical and Critical Dictionary, cited by Holbach as an authority on truth and a forerunner of modern freethought."

memes.label: Piety
memes.description: Piety as external religious performance versus genuine moral virtue; Holbach argues that piety often substitutes for and undermines real ethical conduct.

memes.label: pilgrimage
memes.description: A religious journey to a sacred site; Holbach recounts the story of a hermit who vowed to make a seventh pilgrimage to Mecca, using the narrative to expose the self-deception of those who see divine providence in personal good fortune while ignoring widespread suffering.

memes.label: Place Index
memes.description: Index of all place entries in the wiki — geographical locations, cities, regions, and biblical sites referenced in Holbach's Common Sense and its critique of religious geography.

memes.label: Plato
memes.description: The Athenian idealist philosopher whose "fabulous conjectures" — in Holbach's judgment — seduced the ancient world away from the sensible materialist systems of Democritus and Epicurus, and whose otherworldly metaphysics provided the philosophical template for Christian theology's denigration of the natural world.

memes.label: Pleasure
memes.description: Holbach defends pleasure as a natural good and the legitimate aim of human life; rejecting the ascetic Christian devaluation of earthly enjoyment, he argues that the pursuit of pleasure is not sinful but rational — the natural guide to human well-being and the foundation of secular ethics.

memes.label: Politics
memes.description: Holbach's critique of the alliance between religion and politics — he argues that their union produces despotic rulers, abject subjects, and a "double legislation" fatal to both the people and the sovereign.

memes.label: Politics Index
memes.description: Index of political concepts in the wiki — Holbach's critique of the throne-and-altar alliance and his vision of secular governance.

memes.label: Pontiff
memes.description: In Holbach's critique, the Pontiff represents the ultimate concentration of ecclesiastical power — a "haughty Pontiff" who exercises tyrannical authority over sovereigns and nations alike. (002-291)

memes.label: Poverty
memes.description: Material deprivation that Christian morality perversely valorizes as a spiritual virtue — critiqued by Holbach as a doctrine that serves the wealthy clergy by reconciling the poor to their exploitation.

memes.label: Power
memes.description: Holbach analyzes power as the central mechanism by which religion and despotism jointly oppress humanity, arguing that the division between spiritual and temporal power creates a "double yoke" from which people must liberate themselves through enlightenment.

memes.label: Praise
memes.description: In Holbach's analysis, praise of Providence is a form of flattery directed at an incomprehensible being — a practice that diverts human gratitude away from nature and toward an imaginary recipient of thanks. (002-054)

memes.label: Prayer
memes.description: Prayer as a performative contradiction — Holbach argues that petitioning God for favors implicitly admits dissatisfaction with divine providence and reveals the futility of attempting to alter necessary natural laws.

memes.label: Preach
memes.description: Holbach treats preaching as a central mechanism of religious propagation — the act by which doctrines are disseminated, often by those who fail to practice what they preach, and which depends for its success not on truth but on the credulity of the audience. (002-388)

memes.label: Predestination
memes.description: The theological doctrine of predestination versus Holbach's materialist determinism; predestination makes God the author of sin and renders moral judgment meaningless.

memes.label: Prejudice
memes.description: The uncritical acceptance of inherited beliefs that Holbach identifies as a primary obstacle to rational inquiry, deliberately cultivated by religious and political authorities to maintain control over the human mind.

memes.label: Pride
memes.description: The overvaluation of one's own judgment and status — identified by Holbach as the defining vice of the priesthood and the psychological engine of religious persecution, dogmatic certainty, and the refusal to admit ignorance about ultimate questions.

memes.label: Priest
memes.description: The class of religious functionaries whom Holbach identifies as self-interested charlatans who exploit credulity and fear to dominate the ignorant and support despotism.

memes.label: Priestcraft
memes.description: The systematic manipulation of religious belief by a professional clergy for the purposes of power, wealth, and social control — Holbach's central critical concept, distinct from the individual priest (a person) and the priesthood (an institution).

memes.label: Priesthood
memes.description: The institutional office of religious mediation, which Holbach analyzes in "Common Sense" as a self-authorizing system that places priests above sovereigns and laws; the priesthood is the structural apparatus by which an invisible deity's "ministers" extract power, wealth, and deference from populations denied direct access to divine truth.

memes.label: Principle
memes.description: A fundamental proposition from which other truths are derived — Holbach argues that religious principles are drawn from ignorance rather than experience, and that true principles must be grounded in the observation of nature, not in inherited authority.

memes.label: Probability
memes.description: Holbach's materialist critique of "chance" as a word covering human ignorance of natural causes — in a deterministic universe governed by fixed laws, nothing occurs by accident and probability is merely the measure of our incomplete knowledge.

memes.label: Problem of Evil
memes.description: The empirical refutation of divine goodness based on the observable existence of suffering — Holbach argues evil is evidence that the God-hypothesis is false, not a puzzle to be reconciled with it.

memes.label: Procession
memes.description: Holbach lists religious processions among the external rituals that the clergy prescribe as remedies for calamities — external ceremonies that, in his analysis, distract from genuine moral conduct and rational inquiry. (002-442)

memes.label: Progress
memes.description: The forward movement of human knowledge and society through the application of reason — which Holbach argues is obstructed by theology and enabled by the rejection of superstition.

memes.label: Promise
