memes.description: In Holbach's analysis, divine promises — of paradise, salvation, and remission of sins — are instruments of psychological manipulation that exploit human hope to secure obedience, while never being honored in this life. (002-094)

memes.label: Proof
memes.description: The rational demonstration of truth through evidence and logic — Holbach demands proof for theological claims and finds none, concluding that religion rests on assertion rather than demonstration.

memes.label: Prophecy
memes.description: Holbach's naturalistic critique of prophecy as the product of visionary fanaticism or deliberate imposture, not divine foreknowledge.

memes.label: Prophet
memes.description: Holbach treats prophets as a category of religious imposture, distinguishing between sincere self-deceivers and conscious frauds; he examines the Hebrew prophets as legislators who shaped religion for political ends. (002-465)(002-438)

memes.label: Providence
memes.description: The doctrine that God actively cares for and governs human affairs, which Holbach demolishes by appeal to the observable misery and injustice of the world.

memes.label: Punishment
memes.description: The theological doctrine of divine retribution — which Holbach demonstrates is incompatible with divine goodness, foreknowledge, and omnipotence, and functions primarily as a tool of psychological coercion rather than a principle of justice.

memes.label: Pythagoras
memes.description: Ancient Greek philosopher (c. 570–495 BCE) whose disciples' unquestioning faith in his authority Holbach cites as an analogy for religious credulity; also grouped with Plato among ancient thinkers who sought wisdom through priestly initiation and mystery cults. (002-274)(002-435)

memes.label: Quality Showcase
memes.description: The 100 highest-quality entry pages in the wiki, ranked by computed quality score — a showcase of the most polished, well-structured, and thoroughly cited content across all types.

memes.label: Reason
memes.description: The human faculty of logical thought and empirical judgment which Holbach champions as the sole reliable guide to truth, in opposition to religious faith and supernatural revelation.

memes.label: Reform
memes.description: The improvement of social, political, and religious institutions through rational critique — which Holbach argues is both necessary and possible once superstition is abandoned.

memes.label: The Reformation
memes.description: The Reformation (16th century), a religious movement that fractured Western Christianity; criticized in Holbach's "Common Sense" as an incomplete break from superstition.

memes.label: Relic
memes.description: Holbach includes the veneration of relics among the fraudulent practices — alongside image worship — by which religious systems exploit the credulity of believers, treating objects associated with holy figures as conduits of supernatural power. (003-021)

memes.label: Religion
memes.description: The organized system of beliefs, rituals, and moral codes centered on a deity or deities; Holbach critiques religion in "Common Sense" as a human invention that serves priestly power rather than divine truth.

memes.label: Remorse
memes.description: Remorse as a natural psychological phenomenon rather than a divine voice, grounded in social consciousness and self-awareness.

memes.label: René Descartes
memes.description: French rationalist philosopher (1596–1650) whose system was studied by Jean Meslier and whom Holbach cites as an example of a great mind compelled to "invent hypotheses and evasions" to reconcile reason with theological dogma.

memes.label: Repentance
memes.description: Holbach critiques the doctrine of repentance as a convenient loophole in religious morality — sinners are easily reconciled through tears and ritual without any real amendment of conduct or reparation to those they have harmed. (002-347)(002-376)

memes.label: Resurrection of Jesus
memes.description: The Resurrection of Jesus, the central miracle claim of Christianity; critiqued in Holbach's "Common Sense" through exposure of Gospel contradictions and rational scrutiny.

memes.label: Revelation
memes.description: The claim that a deity has directly communicated knowledge to select human intermediaries, which Holbach argues is logically incoherent (a just God would reveal Himself to all, not a favored few) and historically suspect (every revelation contradicts every other).

memes.label: Revolution
memes.description: The overthrow of established political and intellectual authority; Holbach's "Common Sense" (1772) advocates a revolution in human thinking — a rejection of religious and political dogmas in favor of reason and nature — which would find its political expression in the French Revolution (1789).

memes.label: Reward
memes.description: The theological doctrine that God dispenses rewards for virtuous conduct — a mechanism Holbach dismantles by showing that divine reward is incoherent under the premises of predestination, grace, and human dependence on an omnipotent creator.

memes.label: Rights
memes.description: Entitlements grounded in nature and social contract — Holbach argues that "divine rights" are a theological fiction used to justify tyranny, while natural rights derive from the real interests and needs of human beings living in society.

memes.label: Ritual
memes.description: Formalized religious acts and ceremonies that Holbach argues are instruments of priestly control, bearing "the seal of stupidity or barbarity" and serving to distract from rational inquiry.

memes.label: Roman Empire
memes.description: The ancient Mediterranean empire within which Christianity spread from a provincial sect to an imperial religion; Holbach references the Roman Empire in "Common Sense" as the political machine that Christianity co-opted, and as an example of how religions are shaped by the political structures they inhabit.

memes.label: Rome
memes.description: Rome, the capital of Italy and the seat of the Papacy; in Holbach's "Common Sense," a symbol of the institutional power and historical reach of organized religion.

memes.label: Sacrament
memes.description: Religious sacraments as empty ceremonies claiming supernatural efficacy; Holbach critiques sacramental theology through Meslier's testimony and gospel contradictions.

memes.label: Sacred
memes.description: The quality of being set apart from rational scrutiny — Holbach argues that nothing is inherently sacred; the label is a human invention designed to place certain ideas and institutions beyond criticism, and the greatest crimes have been committed under its protection.

memes.label: Sacrifice
memes.description: Holbach denounces sacrifice as a pernicious religious institution that substitutes ritual payment for genuine moral conduct; the logic of sacrifice — appeasing an angry deity through offerings — corrupts both the concept of God and the foundations of ethics.

memes.label: Saint
memes.description: The Catholic category of exceptionally holy persons who serve as intermediaries between humanity and God — critiqued by Holbach as a multiplication of supernatural beings that fragments worship and serves priestly authority.

memes.label: Salvation
memes.description: The religious doctrine that divine intervention can rescue human beings from sin and eternal punishment, which Holbach critiques as a manufactured anxiety used by priests to secure obedience and material advantage.

memes.label: Schism
memes.description: The formal division of a religious body into separate, mutually hostile organizations; Holbach treats schisms not as theological divergences but as inevitable products of a system in which authority rests on unverifiable claims interpreted by competing human elites.

memes.label: Schismatic
memes.description: Holbach identifies schism as a recurring pattern in religious history — from Moses described as "an Egyptian schismatic" to Christianity itself characterized as "a reformed Judaism" — treating religious innovation as fundamentally schismatic rather than revelatory. (002-439)

memes.label: Science
memes.description: The systematic pursuit of knowledge through observation and reason, which Holbach champions as the intellectual alternative to theology and the engine of human progress.

memes.label: Scripture
memes.description: Holbach's critique of sacred texts as human-authored documents lacking divine authority, deployed to undermine revealed religion's epistemic foundations.

memes.label: Secrecy
memes.description: Secrecy as a mechanism of priestly power; Holbach argues that religious mysteries are deliberately concealed from rational scrutiny to maintain clerical authority over the ignorant.

memes.label: Sect
memes.description: A sub-division within a religion defined by doctrinal difference, which Holbach treats as evidence of religion's human origin — if God revealed truth, competing sects would not exist.

relations.label: Revocation of the Edict of Nantes

memes.label: Sensation
memes.description: For Holbach, all human knowledge originates in sensory experience; the senses are the only reliable guides to truth, and the theological demand to sacrifice reason and distrust the senses is an attack on the foundations of human understanding.

memes.label: Senses
memes.description: The sensory faculties through which humans acquire all knowledge, forming the empiricist foundation of Holbach's materialist epistemology in "Common Sense."

memes.label: Sentient Being
memes.description: The nature of sentient beings in Holbach's materialist philosophy; consciousness without soul, suffering as the foundation of morality, and the argument from universal suffering against a benevolent God.

memes.label: Sermon
memes.description: Holbach treats the sermon as an instrument of priestly control — a performance that substitutes Latin erudition and rhetorical display for genuine moral instruction, keeping the laity in ignorance rather than enlightening them. (002-211)(002-431)

memes.label: serpent
memes.description: The Eden serpent as paradigmatic biblical mythology — Holbach exposes the talking serpent as a narrative device from ancient Near Eastern folklore, not evidence of divine inspiration.

memes.label: simplicity
memes.description: Foolish simplicity — Holbach's term for the credulity of believers who call God good while attributing calamities like pestilence, war, and famine to Him, a plain contradiction. (002-153)

memes.label: Sin
memes.description: The theological category of moral offense against God, which Holbach dismantles as an incoherent invention of priestcraft used to enslave minds through guilt and fear.

memes.label: Skeptic
memes.description: Holbach defends religious skepticism not as a moral failing but as an effect of rational examination — arguing that all honest inquirers are either skeptics or atheists rather than genuine believers. (002-236)

memes.label: Skepticism
memes.description: Philosophical skepticism as deployed by Holbach against religious claims — the demand for evidence before belief, and the refusal to accept authority without demonstration.

memes.label: Society
memes.description: The collective organization of human beings, which Holbach argues is the natural condition of humanity and the proper object of political and moral concern, not an afterthought to individual salvation.

memes.label: Socrates
memes.description: "Greek philosopher (c. 470–399 BCE), founder of Western moral philosophy, whose idealism is contrasted with materialist philosophy by Holbach."

memes.label: Solomon
memes.description: King of Israel, son of David, builder of the First Temple and reputed author of Proverbs, Ecclesiastes, and the Wisdom of Solomon — whom Holbach cites as the Bible's own internal skeptic, doubting the immortality of the soul.

memes.label: Soul
memes.description: The alleged immaterial and immortal component of human beings; Holbach argues the concept is "an idea without a model," a theological fiction with no empirical basis, sustained by the fear of death and priestly exploitation.

memes.label: Sovereign
memes.description: The concept of absolute rulership applied to God and earthly monarchs, which Holbach argues is indistinguishable from despotism and unworthy of rational allegiance.

memes.label: Spinoza
memes.description: Baruch Spinoza (1632–1677), Dutch rationalist philosopher whom Holbach groups with Hobbes and Bayle among the modern thinkers who followed the path of freethought against religious orthodoxy. (002-448)

memes.label: Spirit
memes.description: The supposed immaterial substance, which Holbach argues is an empty word devoid of meaning — a linguistic placeholder that theology uses to avoid the material implications of rational inquiry.

memes.label: Submission
memes.description: In Holbach's critique of religion, submission — particularly blind submission to priests and the submission demanded by religious authority — is analyzed as the core mechanism by which religion maintains control over its adherents. (002-265)

memes.label: Substance
memes.description: The fundamental material reality underlying all things; Holbach argues that substance is exclusively material — "spirit" as immaterial substance is an idea without a model, an absence of thought.

memes.label: Suffering
memes.description: The universal human experience of physical and mental pain, which Holbach deploys as a decisive empirical argument against the existence of a benevolent and omnipotent deity.

memes.label: Supernatural
memes.description: The realm of beings, forces, and events claimed to exist beyond nature and natural laws, systematically critiqued by Holbach as an empty category produced by ignorance and priestly deception.

memes.label: Superstition
memes.description: The cowardly and inordinate fear of divinity that Holbach identifies as the psychological foundation of all religious systems, indistinguishable from religion itself in its effects on human conduct.

memes.label: Temperament
memes.description: In Holbach's materialist anthropology, the innate physical constitution determining all passions, desires, and actions — the material substrate of character that refutes free will.

memes.label: Temperance
memes.description: The virtue of moderation and self-restraint, especially regarding desires and appetites; in Holbach's "Common Sense," the claim that religion promotes temperance is rejected as empirically false.

memes.label: Temple
memes.description: Religious temples as sites of superstitious practice; Holbach uses pagan and Christian temple miracles as comparative evidence that all religions share the same structure of credulity.

memes.label: Temporal Power
memes.description: In Holbach's political philosophy, temporal power refers to the legitimate civil authority of sovereigns and the state, which is perpetually undermined and rivaled by the spiritual power claimed by religious institutions and their clergy.

memes.label: Terror
memes.description: Religious terror as the psychological instrument by which priests and rulers subjugate populations — Holbach argues that fear of invisible powers paralyzes reason and sustains despotism.

memes.label: Testament
memes.description: Holbach's critique of the Old and New Testaments as contradictory, historically unreliable human documents that cannot serve as the foundation for religious belief or moral authority.

memes.label: Testimony
memes.description: In Holbach's epistemology, the reliability of claims depends on the nature of their source — the testimony of the senses provides the only solid foundation for knowledge, while the testimony of interested parties in matters of religion is inherently unreliable (002-019; 002-246).

memes.label: Theism
memes.description: Belief in a personal God who created and governs the world; Holbach argues that theism is no more rational than superstition — its God is equally contradictory and its "natural religion" collapses under scrutiny.

memes.label: Theology
memes.description: The systematic attempt to reason about God and divine matters, which Holbach argues is an elaborate exercise in self-deception — a discipline that produces only "an absence of ideas."

memes.label: Theology Index
memes.description: Index of theological concepts and doctrines discussed in Holbach's Common Sense — grouped by themes of deity, revelation, scripture, ritual, and religious institutions.

memes.label: thomas-woolston
memes.description: Thomas Woolston (1668–1733), English Deist and freethinker celebrated by Holbach as a courageous predecessor in the rational critique of Christianity and its miraculous claims.

memes.label: Thought
memes.description: Holbach champions thought — the exercise of critical reason and reflection — as the sole reliable instrument for liberating humanity from religious prejudice; thinking is the distinguishing activity of the philosopher, the precondition of moral and intellectual emancipation.

memes.label: throne-and-altar
memes.description: Holbach's central political metaphor for the strategic alliance between monarchy and church — mutually reinforcing systems of oppression in which the throne provides coercive force and the altar provides divine legitimation.

memes.label: Time
memes.description: Holbach critiques theological conceptions of eternity and eternal punishment as logically incoherent and morally abhorrent, arguing that the notion of an eternal being inflicting eternal torments on finite creatures reveals religion's true character as an instrument of terror.

memes.label: tolerance
memes.description: True tolerance — which Holbach argues cannot exist under religion, because "everywhere a jealous God" commands persecution, undermining sociability, tolerance, and humanity, "these first virtues." (002-321)

memes.label: Toleration
memes.description: The principle of permitting religious beliefs and practices different from one's own — Holbach argues that toleration is the only rational response to religious diversity and that it follows necessarily from materialist [[determinism]], since no one chooses their beliefs voluntarily.

memes.label: Torture
memes.description: The infliction of severe physical and psychological suffering, especially as a tool of religious persecution — Holbach argues that when princes torture heretics at the urging of priests, they commit atrocities in the name of a God they claim is good, revealing the moral bankruptcy of revealed religion.

memes.label: Transfiguration of Jesus
memes.description: The Transfiguration of Jesus — a Gospel event whose chronology Holbach uses to expose contradictions in the narrative timeline and the composite, human origin of the New Testament.

memes.label: Transubstantiation
memes.description: The Catholic doctrine that bread and wine become the literal body and blood of Christ during the Mass — Holbach treats transubstantiation as the paradigmatic example of religious credulity, a belief so absurd that even other Christian denominations ridicule it, yet one that reveals the psychological mechanisms by which all religious [[dogma]] secures assent.

memes.label: Trinity
memes.description: The Trinity as a paradigm case of theological absurdity in Holbach's critique; the "ineffable mystery" used as comparative evidence that all religions demand belief in logical contradictions.

memes.label: Truth
memes.description: The correspondence between human understanding and reality, which Holbach presents as the natural goal of reason and the victim of religious deception.

memes.label: Tyranny
memes.description: The system of absolute and arbitrary rule that Holbach identifies as both a consequence of religious superstition and its political enforcer, maintained through the systematic suppression of reason and freedom.

memes.label: Tyrant
memes.description: The tyrant as Holbach's model of divine character — an absolute ruler whose arbitrary power, cruelty, and indifference to suffering reveal the moral bankruptcy of theological conceptions of God

memes.label: "Understanding"
memes.description: "In Holbach's materialist epistemology, understanding is the natural human capacity for rational comprehension grounded in sensory experience — contrasted sharply with theological claims that divine truths transcend human reason."

memes.label: Universe
memes.description: The totality of all that exists; Holbach argues the universe is eternal, uncreated, necessarily existent, and self-organizing — requiring no divine creator or external intelligence to account for its order.

memes.label: Utility
memes.description: The principle that actions and institutions should be judged by their contribution to human welfare — Holbach grounds morality in social utility rather than divine command, anticipating utilitarian ethics.

memes.label: Vanity
memes.description: The human tendency to overestimate one's own importance — identified by Holbach as a primary psychological source of religious delusion, the belief that the universe was made for man, and the engine of priestly corruption and persecution.

memes.label: Vice
memes.description: Conduct that causes harm to oneself or others — Holbach redefines vice in secular terms as a failure of enlightened self-interest rather than a transgression against divine law, arguing that religion's concept of sin obscures the real social causes and consequences of harmful behavior.

memes.label: Virtue
memes.description: Moral excellence conceived without supernatural foundation, which Holbach argues is the natural product of reason applied to human needs and social relations, not a gift of divine grace.

memes.label: Voltaire
memes.description: François-Marie Arouet (1694–1778), known as Voltaire, the preeminent figure of the French Enlightenment whose critiques of religious intolerance, clerical power, and superstition supplied key arguments that Holbach radicalized in "Common Sense."

memes.label: war
memes.description: War, alongside pestilence and famine, is one of the calamities Holbach cites as evidence against the existence of a benevolent Providence — a "good Lord" who nonetheless sends war and devastation.

memes.label: Weakness
memes.description: Human weakness as the psychological foundation of religion — Holbach argues that charlatans exploit natural human frailty to sell supernatural remedies, and that religious belief persists because men are too weak to examine their own fears.

memes.label: Wealth
memes.description: Material abundance analyzed by Holbach as a natural human good that religion perversely devalues while the clergy systematically accumulate it — exposing the hypocrisy of otherworldly morality.

memes.label: Will
memes.description: The human faculty of choice and volition, which Holbach argues is entirely determined by motives, temperament, and external circumstances, not by a free and undetermined spiritual power.

memes.label: Wisdom
memes.description: The capacity for sound judgment based on knowledge, experience, and reason, which Holbach contrasts with the "divine wisdom" invoked by theologians — an inscrutable attribute that serves as a rhetorical refuge whenever theological claims are exposed as absurd by rational examination.

memes.label: Thomas Woolston
memes.description: "English theologian and freethinker (1668–1733), author of Discourses on the Miracles, praised by Holbach for his public challenge to Christian miracle claims."

memes.label: Worship
memes.description: The act of venerating or adoring a deity through ritual, prayer, and sacrifice, which Holbach argues is a practice directed at a fiction — an "idle fancy" whose attributed qualities make it unworthy of worship even if it existed.

memes.label: Zeal
memes.description: Fervent and uncritical commitment to religious doctrine — Holbach argues that zeal is not a virtue but a dangerous passion, rooted in ignorance and credulity, that has caused immense human suffering throughout history.

relations.label: Revocation of the Edict of Nantes

memes.label: Zealot
memes.description: In Holbach's critique, the zealot represents religious devotion taken to its logical extreme — fanatical commitment that subverts reason and humanity in the name of faith. (002-275)

memes.label: Zeno of Citium
memes.description: Founder of Stoicism (c. 334–262 BCE) whose "fabulous conjectures" — in Holbach's judgment — helped displace the sensible materialist systems of the ancient atomists in favor of theological philosophy.
